explanation: Meta-prompting is the technique of using an LLM to generate or refine your own prompts before running the final task. Instead of spending hours trial-and-erroring prompts, ask the model to write a better prompt for you, then use that improved prompt for the real task. This often produces dramatically better outputs with less effort.

title: Definition and benefits: better prompts, reduced trial-and-error
Workflow: ask model to generate or refine the prompt before the final run
Example: generate a prompt for product description or SEO-optimized blog
Prompt chaining (stepwise task decomposition)
explanation: Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into a sequence of simpler prompts, where each step's output becomes the next step's input. Instead of asking the model to do everything in one prompt (which often produces mediocre results), you guide it step by step. This produces higher quality, more coherent results for complex multi-stage tasks.

title: Technique: split complex tasks into sequential prompts
Implementation steps: produce intermediate outputs and feed them forward
Example chain: summarize topic -> generate exam questions -> compile revision sheet
Benefits: improved coherence and controllable multi-step outputs
PromptBase marketplace and prompt reuse
explanation: PromptBase is an online marketplace where you can buy, sell, and share high-quality prompts. Instead of spending hours crafting the perfect prompt for a specific task, you can find one that experts have already tested and optimized. It covers prompts for image generation, copywriting, coding, education, and more.

title: Purpose: buy/sell high-quality prompts and access free prompts
Getting started: create account, upload tested prompts with keywords
Best practices: use clear descriptions and relevant metadata for discoverability
General effective prompting tips
explanation: Effective prompting is a skill that improves with practice. Key principles: be specific about what you want, provide context about the audience and purpose, give exact format instructions, and iterate based on results. These tips apply regardless of which model or tool you use and are the foundation of good prompt engineering.

title: Clarity and specificity: give exact length, tone, and format requirements
Contextual awareness: provide audience, role, and background information
Output guidance: instruct exact structure (tables, bullet points, examples)
Adaptability: use reusable templates and iterate with feedback
What is an LLM
explanation: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a type of AI system trained on massive amounts of text data to understand and generate human language. LLMs have billions of parameters (learned numeric weights) and require powerful computers to train and run. They can generate text, answer questions, summarize documents, translate languages, and write code.

title: int main(), return 0, braces and semicolons
Core principle: next-word prediction
explanation: The fundamental task LLMs are trained on is next-word (actually next-token) prediction. Given a sequence of tokens, predict the most probable next token. Repeat this process and you get a full generated sentence. This simple objective, applied to massive data with massive compute, produces models with surprising reasoning and generation capabilities.

title: Transformer architecture overview
explanation: Transformers are the neural network architecture that powers all modern LLMs. Introduced in 2017, they replaced older RNN-based architectures. The key innovation is the attention mechanism, which lets the model process all tokens in parallel and capture relationships between any two tokens regardless of their distance in the sequence.

title: Parallel processing and long-range dependency handling
Pre-processing: tokenization
explanation: Before text enters the model, it must be converted into tokens — numeric IDs the model can process. Tokenization splits text into pieces (whole words, subwords, or characters) and maps each piece to an ID in the model's vocabulary. The choice of tokenization strategy affects model efficiency and how it handles rare words.

title: How tokens are split and tokenizer variability
Impact on input length and model behavior
Embeddings and positional encoding
explanation: After tokenization, each token ID is converted into a dense vector (embedding) that represents its meaning numerically. These vectors place similar-meaning words close together in a high-dimensional space. Since the Transformer processes all tokens simultaneously and has no inherent sense of order, positional encodings are added to tell the model where in the sequence each token appears.

title: Positional encodings to preserve word order
Encoder internals
explanation: The Transformer encoder reads the input sequence and produces a rich, context-aware representation of each token. It uses multi-head self-attention to capture relationships between all tokens, then a feed-forward network to process each token independently. Add & Norm layers stabilize training throughout. The encoder output represents the input's full meaning.

title: Add & Norm and feed-forward network roles
Decoder internals
explanation: The Transformer decoder generates output tokens one at a time. It has masked self-attention (to prevent looking at future tokens), cross-attention (to read the encoder's output), and a feed-forward network. This structure lets the decoder generate contextually coherent outputs that are grounded in the input processed by the encoder.

title: Add & Norm plus FFN for generation
End-to-end generation flow
explanation: Putting it all together: input text is tokenized, embedded, and passed through the encoder to produce contextual representations. The decoder uses these representations plus any previously generated tokens to predict the next token. This process repeats until an end token is generated. The final token sequence is decoded back to text.

title: Input processing: tokenization -> embeddings -> positional encoding
Decoder generates tokens sequentially and converts via linear+softmax
Future trends and concerns
explanation: LLMs are evolving rapidly. Context windows are growing from thousands to millions of tokens. Models are becoming multimodal (understanding images, audio, and video alongside text). Reasoning capabilities are improving through techniques like chain-of-thought. Alongside these advances, serious ethical concerns around bias, hallucinations, and misuse require ongoing attention.

title: Expanding context windows and multimodal inputs
Introduction to C++
explanation: C++ is a general-purpose programming language that adds object-oriented features to C. It is widely used in system software, game engines, and competitive programming. For example, the Unreal Engine is written in C++. Learning C++ gives you a strong foundation for understanding how computers work at a lower level.

title: What is C++ and common use cases
C++ as extension of C and OOP overview
Relevance in interviews and system programming
Basic Syntax and Program Structure
explanation: Every C++ program has a defined skeleton: headers bring in libraries, int main() is the entry point, and statements end with semicolons. For example, a minimal program includes #include<iostream>, int main(){}, and return 0. Getting this structure right is the first step to writing any working program.

title: using namespace std; and compilation flow
Comments and Simple I/O
explanation: Comments help explain code to readers without affecting execution. Output is done with cout and << . For example, cout << "Hello" << endl; prints Hello and moves to the next line. Good comments and clear output make programs easy to read and debug.

title: Input Methods
explanation: C++ reads user input using cin or getline. cin reads whitespace-delimited tokens while getline reads an entire line including spaces. For example, if a user types John Doe, cin >> name captures only John while getline(cin, name) captures John Doe.

title: Data Types and Formatting
explanation: C++ has several primitive types to store different kinds of data: int for whole numbers, float/double for decimals, char for characters, and bool for true/false. For example, double pi = 3.14159; stores a precise decimal value. Choosing the right type saves memory and prevents bugs.

title: Primitive types: int, float, double, char, bool, long long
fixed and setprecision() for float formatting
Operators and Expressions
explanation: Operators perform computations on values. C++ has arithmetic (+,-,*,/,%), relational (==,!=,<,>), and logical (&&,||,!) operators. For example, 10 % 3 gives 1 (the remainder). Combining operators forms expressions that are the building blocks of all logic.

title: Type Conversion and Casting
explanation: Type conversion changes a value from one type to another. Implicit conversion happens automatically (int to double), while explicit casting forces the change. For example, (double)5/2 gives 2.5 instead of 2. String-to-number functions like stoi() are essential when reading numeric input as text.

title: to_string, stoi, stod, stof, stoll for string conversions
Map
explanation: std::map stores key-value pairs with keys in sorted order and uniqueness enforced. Think of it like a dictionary — each word (key) maps to one definition (value). For example, map<string,int> wordCount; lets you count how many times each word appears in text, with keys always in alphabetical order.

title: Key-value container with sorted unique keys
Multimap
explanation: multimap is like map but allows duplicate keys. For example, storing student scores where multiple students can have the same score. All entries remain sorted by key. Unlike map, operator[] is not available because a key can map to multiple values.

title: find vs equal_range to get all matches
Unordered Map
explanation: unordered_map uses a hash table for O(1) average-case insert, lookup, and delete — much faster than map's O(log n). For example, counting character frequencies in a string is very fast with unordered_map. The trade-off: keys are unordered and worst-case hash collisions make it O(n).

title: Sorting (std::sort)
explanation: std::sort from <algorithm> sorts a range in-place using introsort (hybrid quicksort/heapsort), giving O(n log n) time. By default it sorts ascending. For example, sort(v.begin(), v.end()); sorts a vector of integers from smallest to largest in one line.

title: Sorting 2D vectors and by specific columns
Max/Min and find utilities
explanation: STL provides max_element, min_element, and find in <algorithm> to locate values in a range. They all return iterators, which you dereference to get values. For example, *max_element(v.begin(),v.end()) gives the largest element in a vector in O(n) time.

title: find algorithm and return iterator semantics
Permutations (next_permutation / prev_permutation)
explanation: next_permutation rearranges a range to its next lexicographic permutation and returns true; returns false if it was already the last permutation (and wraps to the first). For example, sorted array {1,2,3} followed by repeated next_permutation calls generates all 6 permutations of those three numbers.

title: Generate next and previous lexicographic permutations
Using sort + next_permutation to list all permutations
Return values and edge cases (already max/min)
Deque
explanation: deque (double-ended queue) supports fast O(1) insert and remove at both front and back, unlike vector which only supports fast back operations. Think of it as a queue where you can also add/remove from the front efficiently. Used in sliding window algorithms and BFS implementations.

title: Stack
explanation: A stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) — the last element pushed is the first popped. Think of a stack of plates: you always take the top one. In C++, std::stack wraps a deque by default. Common uses: function call management, expression evaluation, and undo operations.

title: Queue
explanation: A queue follows FIFO (First In, First Out) — the first element added is the first removed. Think of a line at a ticket counter. In C++, std::queue wraps a deque. Used in BFS, task scheduling, and printer spooling where order of arrival matters.

title: Priority Queue
explanation: A priority_queue is a heap-based container where the highest-priority element is always at the top. By default it is a max-heap (largest element first). For example, in a task scheduler, highest-priority tasks are always processed first regardless of insertion order.

title: Set
explanation: std::set stores unique elements in sorted ascending order using a red-black tree. Insertions, lookups, and deletions are O(log n). For example, set<int> seen; seen.insert(x); if(seen.count(x)) checks if a number was already encountered — useful for deduplication.

title: Ordered set: uniqueness and sorted order
Multiset
explanation: multiset stores elements in sorted order like set, but allows duplicate values. For example, multiset<int> ms; ms.insert(3); ms.insert(3); stores two 3s. Useful when you need sorted order while tracking how many times each value appears.

title: insert and erase (single vs all occurrences)
Unordered Set
explanation: unordered_set stores unique elements using a hash table for O(1) average insert, lookup, and delete — faster than set's O(log n). For example, checking if a number was seen before in an array runs in O(1) per lookup, making the total algorithm O(n).

title: What is DSA
explanation: DSA stands for Data Structures and Algorithms. Data Structures organize data (like arrays or trees), while Algorithms are step-by-step procedures to solve problems using that data. Together they are the core of efficient programming. For example, a search engine uses hash maps (structure) and sorting algorithms to return results in milliseconds.

title: Definition of Data Structures and Algorithms
Role of DSA in efficient problem solving
Prime: only factors 1 and itself; 1 is not prime
Data Structures: concept and examples
explanation: A data structure is a container that organizes data for efficient storage and retrieval. Like a bookshelf organizes books by genre, each data structure fits certain tasks. Arrays are great for indexed access; linked lists for dynamic insertion. Choosing the right structure is as important as the algorithm itself.

title: Common examples: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs
Algorithms: concept and examples
explanation: An algorithm is a step-by-step recipe to solve a specific problem. Like a cooking recipe that guarantees the same dish every time if followed correctly, a good algorithm always produces the correct answer in a predictable number of steps. Sorting and searching are the most fundamental algorithm families.

title: Real-world applications of DSA
explanation: DSA powers everyday technology. Google's search ranks billions of pages using graph algorithms. Facebook's friend suggestions use graph traversal. Amazon's recommendation engine uses trees and hashing. Without efficient DSA, these services would be impossibly slow at their scale.

title: Benefits of learning DSA
explanation: Mastering DSA makes you a better programmer across all languages and domains. It sharpens logical thinking, helps you write faster and cleaner code, and is essential for passing technical interviews at top companies. The skills transfer — a tree traversal learned in C++ applies directly in Python or Java.

title: Transferable skills across languages and domains
Definition and components
explanation: Space complexity measures the total memory an algorithm uses as its input size grows. It includes input space, auxiliary space (extra memory the algorithm allocates), and stack space (from recursive calls). For example, creating a copy array of size n adds O(n) auxiliary space even if the algorithm itself is otherwise constant.

title: Total memory used as input size grows
Input space, auxiliary space, and stack space
Examples and analysis
explanation: Analyzing space means identifying where memory is allocated. A simple addition function uses O(1) — just a few variables. Summing an array still uses O(1) auxiliary space even though the input is O(n). Recursion adds stack space proportional to call depth, which must be accounted for separately.

title: Sum of array: O(n) input space + O(1) auxiliary space
Identify where extra arrays or recursion add space
Notation and best case
explanation: Space complexity uses the same Big-O notation as time complexity. O(1) is constant space — ideal. O(n) grows linearly with input. O(log n) occurs in balanced recursion (like binary search). The goal is always to minimize space usage, especially in memory-constrained environments.

title: O(n), O(log n), O(n^2) common space classes
Practical considerations
explanation: In practice, we usually measure auxiliary space (extra memory beyond the input) rather than total space. Modifying the input in-place avoids extra allocation. Recursion can silently add O(n) stack space — sometimes an iterative approach is preferred to keep space O(1).

title: Typically ignore space for input/output unless required
Distinguish between modifying input vs extra allocation
Definition and purpose
explanation: Time complexity measures how the number of operations an algorithm performs grows as the input size n increases. It doesn't measure clock time — it counts operations. For example, a loop that runs n times has O(n) complexity. This lets us compare algorithms fairly regardless of hardware.

title: Measure algorithm efficiency by input size
Counting operations and examples
explanation: To find time complexity, count how many times the core operation executes as a function of n. A single loop gives O(n). Two nested loops give O(n^2). An if-statement with no loop is O(1). Practice with worked examples until pattern recognition becomes intuitive.

title: Single for-loop: step-by-step count to linear cost
Complexity notations
explanation: Three notations describe algorithm performance bounds. Big-O (upper bound / worst case) is most commonly used. Big-Omega (lower bound / best case) and Big-Theta (tight bound / exact growth rate) provide more complete pictures. In interviews, Big-O is almost always what's expected.

title: Rules for asymptotic analysis
explanation: Asymptotic analysis uses three simple rules to simplify complexity expressions: drop constants, drop lower-order terms, and keep the dominant term. These rules work because for large n, constants and minor terms become negligible. For example, 100n + 5 simplifies to O(n).

title: Focus on highest-order term for large N
Comparing algorithms
explanation: Different complexity classes have vastly different performance at scale. O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n). A O(n^2) algorithm handling n=10^4 in 1 second may take 10^8 seconds for n=10^6. Choosing the better complexity class is often the single most impactful optimization.

title: Constant vs linear vs quadratic growth
Practical effect of growth rates as N increases
Examples: when O(1) beats O(N) and O(N^2) impact
Definition and purpose
explanation: Pseudo code is a human-readable, language-independent description of an algorithm using plain English mixed with programming-like constructs. It lets you plan logic before worrying about syntax. For example, writing 'for each element in list, if element > max, update max' is pseudocode for finding the maximum.

title: Blueprint to plan logic before coding
Benefits
explanation: Pseudocode separates logical thinking from language-specific syntax, making it easier to design, communicate, and debug algorithms. Teams with mixed language backgrounds can discuss algorithms in pseudocode without language barriers. It also speeds up implementation since the logic is pre-planned.

title: Structure and conventions
explanation: Pseudocode has no universal standard, but common conventions include using CAPS for keywords (BEGIN, END, IF, FOR, WHILE, RETURN), indentation for blocks, and plain English for operations. The goal is maximum clarity. Avoid language-specific syntax — pseudocode should be readable by anyone.

title: Use plain-English steps with loop/conditional constructs
Declare variables, loop ranges, and return values
Example: sum of first N natural numbers
explanation: A classic pseudocode example: initialize sum to 0, loop from 1 to N adding each number, then return sum. This simple example demonstrates variable initialization, a FOR loop, accumulation, and a return statement — the four most common pseudocode building blocks.

title: Loop from 1 to N and accumulate
Modulo arithmetic basics
explanation: The modulo operation (%) returns the remainder after integer division. For example, 10 % 3 = 1 because 10 = 3×3 + 1. Modulo is fundamental in cyclic problems (like clock arithmetic), hashing, and checking even/odd. Understanding how negative values behave in different languages prevents subtle bugs.

title: Modulo identities
explanation: Modulo distributes over addition, subtraction, and multiplication. These identities let you compute modulo of large expressions without overflow. For example, (a + b) % m = ((a % m) + (b % m)) % m — apply modulo to each operand before combining to keep numbers small.

title: Divisors of a number
explanation: To find all divisors of n, iterate from 1 to √n. For each i that divides n, both i and n/i are divisors. This halves the work compared to checking all numbers up to n. For example, divisors of 12: i=1(1,12), i=2(2,6), i=3(3,4) — found in 3 steps instead of 12.

title: Iterate i from 1 to sqrt(n) and add i and n/i
Handle perfect squares to avoid duplicates
Sort and output divisors; complexity O(sqrt(n) + k log k)
Prime number definitions and checks
explanation: A prime number has exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Note: 1 is NOT prime. Checking primality naively tests all numbers up to n, but the optimized check only goes up to √n — if no divisor is found by then, the number is prime.

title: Optimized check O(sqrt(n)): test up to sqrt(n)
Sieve of Eratosthenes
explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes finds all primes up to n efficiently. Start with a boolean array marking all numbers true (prime). For each prime p starting at 2, mark all multiples of p starting from p² as not prime. Result: all unmarked indices are prime. Time O(n log log n), space O(n).

title: Boolean array marking; start marking from p*p
Complexity O(n log log n), space O(n)
Use for listing all primes up to n
GCD / HCF definition and naive method
explanation: GCD (Greatest Common Divisor), also called HCF, is the largest integer that divides both numbers. For example, GCD(12,8) = 4. The naive method iterates from 1 to min(a,b) and tracks the largest common divisor found. Simple but slow for large numbers.

title: Naive iteration 1..min(a,b) to find common divisors
Euclidean algorithm for GCD
explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is a fast, elegant method: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a%b), repeated until b=0. For example, GCD(48,18): 48%18=12 → GCD(18,12): 18%12=6 → GCD(12,6): 12%6=0 → GCD(6,0)=6. This runs in O(log(min(a,b))) time.

title: Least Common Multiple (LCM)
explanation: LCM(a,b) is the smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. For example, LCM(4,6) = 12. The efficient formula is LCM = (a/GCD(a,b)) * b, dividing first to avoid overflow before multiplying. This reduces an O(max(a,b)) naive search to O(log(min(a,b))).

title: Definition: smallest number divisible by both
Naive approach: iterate from max(a,b) upward
Efficient compute via GCD: LCM = a*b / GCD(a,b)
Relation between GCD and LCM
explanation: A fundamental identity: GCD(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b. This means if you know GCD, you can compute LCM without iteration. For example, GCD(12,8)=4 and LCM(12,8)=24: 4×24=96=12×8. This relation simplifies code and avoids overflow when carefully ordered.

title: Prime factor interpretation (min vs max powers)
Use relation to compute LCM reliably
Arrays and Memory Layout
explanation: An array stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. The address of element i is calculated as: base_address + i × element_size. This formula gives O(1) indexed access. For example, an int array at address 1000 stores its second element (index 1) at address 1004.

title: Array as linear sequence with index-based access
Implications for access time and locality
Basic Array Operations and C++ Example
explanation: Arrays in C++ are declared as type name[size]; and initialized with values in braces. Elements are accessed with arr[i] (zero-indexed). You can loop through all elements to print or process them. Knowing index 0 is the first element and index n-1 is the last prevents off-by-one errors.

title: Accessing and updating elements by index
Example: reading and printing array elements
Brute-force vs Optimal Solutions (concepts)
explanation: Brute-force tries every possibility — easy to code but often slow. Optimal solutions use clever algorithms or data structures to reduce operations. For finding the largest element, brute-force might sort the whole array, while optimal does one linear scan. Always aim for the optimal approach in interviews.

title: Brute-force: exhaustive methods, easy to implement
Optimal: minimize time and space using algorithms
Trade-offs in time and space complexity
Finding Largest Element — Brute-force (Sort)
explanation: Sort the array in ascending order. The last element (index n-1) is then the largest. Simple to implement using std::sort. However, sorting takes O(n log n) time and may modify the original array — wasteful if all you need is the maximum.

title: Sort array and return element at index n-1
Pseudo-code steps: sort then access last
Time: O(n log n), Space: in-place vs extra copy
Finding Largest Element — Optimal Linear Scan
explanation: Initialize max to the first element, then scan the rest. If any element is larger than the current max, update max. One pass, O(n) time, O(1) space. For example, in array [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6], max starts at 3 and updates to 4→5→9→9, ending at 9.

title: Initialize max = arr[0], traverse and update if arr[i] > max
Space O(1) and correctness with examples
Problem Statement and Constraints
explanation: Find the second largest distinct element in an array. If no such element exists (e.g., all elements equal), return -1. For example, [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6] → 6 (second largest after 9). Duplicates of the largest don't count as the second largest.

title: Return second largest distinct element or -1
Constraints on n and element values
Brute-force (Sort and Scan Backwards)
explanation: Sort the array ascending. The last element is the maximum. Scan backwards from n-2 until you find an element different from the maximum — that's the second largest. If no such element exists, return -1. Time O(n log n) due to sorting.

title: Scan from n-2 down to 0 to find first value != max
Time O(n log n), space considerations
Two-pass Approach (Find Max then Second)
explanation: Pass 1: find the maximum element. Pass 2: find the largest element strictly less than the maximum. Initialize secondLargest = -1 before pass 2. Two passes, O(n) each, so O(n) total time and O(1) space — better than sorting.

title: Second pass: track largest element < max
One-pass Optimal Approach
explanation: Maintain both max and secondLargest in a single traversal. When a new element is larger than max, move max to secondLargest first, then update max. When an element is between secondLargest and max, update secondLargest only. One pass: O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Maintain max and secondLargest while traversing once
Update secondLargest before updating max when needed
Handle duplicates and equal elements correctly
Single traversal: O(n) time, O(1) space
Edge Cases and Complexity
explanation: Edge cases determine correctness on boundary inputs. All-equal arrays, arrays of size 2, all-negative arrays, and arrays where the maximum appears multiple times all require careful handling. Always test on these before finalizing your solution.

title: Small n behavior and initialization choices
Maximum Consecutive Ones and Finding Missing Number
Maximum Consecutive Ones — Problem & Approach
explanation: Given a binary array, find the length of the longest run of consecutive 1s. For example, [1,1,0,1,1,1] → 3 (the last three 1s). Use a current count and a maximum count. Increment current on 1, reset to 0 on 0, and update maximum after each increment.

title: Count current streak when element is 1
Track and update maximum streak seen
Example and O(n) time, O(1) space
Missing Number — Brute-force and Checklist
explanation: Given an array of n-1 distinct integers from 1 to n, find the missing one. Brute-force: for each number 1 to n, search the array — O(n^2). Checklist: create a boolean array, mark present elements, find the unmarked index — O(n) time, O(n) space.

title: Brute-force: check each number 1..n by searching array (O(n^2))
Checklist (temp array) marking presence (O(n) time, O(n) space)
Return index not marked as present
Missing Number — Sum Formula Method
explanation: The sum of 1 to n is n*(n+1)/2. Subtract the sum of the given array from this expected sum. The difference is the missing number. For example, n=4, arr=[1,2,4]: expected=10, actual=7, missing=3. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Subtract sum of array elements to get missing number
Use larger integer type (long long) to avoid overflow
Missing Number — XOR Method and Analysis
explanation: XOR of a number with itself is 0; XOR with 0 is the number itself. XOR all elements in the array, then XOR all numbers 1..n. Paired numbers cancel out, leaving only the missing number. O(n) time, O(1) space — no overflow risk unlike the sum method.

title: XOR all array elements and XOR numbers 1..n to cancel pairs
Final XOR yields the missing value
O(n) time, O(1) space and constant memory
Include n explicitly since array has n-1 elements
Checking if Array is Sorted and Removing Duplicates
Check if array is sorted (ascending)
explanation: Scan the array once. If any element is greater than the next, the array is not sorted. If the full scan completes without finding such a pair, the array is sorted. For example, [1,2,3,4] is sorted; [1,3,2,4] is not (3 > 2 at index 1).

title: Remove duplicates — brute force (set)
explanation: Insert all array elements into an unordered_set (which ignores duplicates automatically). Then copy unique elements back into the array. The size of the set is k (unique count). Return k. Time O(n) average, Space O(n) for the set.

title: Use unordered_set to collect unique values
Overwrite original array with set elements
Remove duplicates — optimal in-place
explanation: Use two pointers on a sorted array. Pointer i tracks where the last unique element was placed. Pointer j scans forward. When arr[j] ≠ arr[i], copy arr[j] to arr[i+1] and advance i. Return i+1 as the count of unique elements. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Two-pointer technique (i tracks last unique, j scans)
Place new unique at arr[i+1] and increment i
Edge cases and return convention
explanation: The array must be sorted for the optimal two-pointer approach. Edge cases include all-duplicate arrays (returns 1) and all-unique arrays (returns n). The return value k means the first k elements of the modified array are the unique elements.

title: Return value k = number of unique elements
Move zeros to end — problem and examples
explanation: Rearrange the array so all zeros are at the end while non-zero elements maintain their original relative order. For example, [0,1,0,3,12] → [1,3,12,0,0]. This must be done in-place. Relative order of non-zeros must be preserved — this rules out simple sorting.

title: Maintain relative order of non-zero elements
Brute-force approach (temporary list)
explanation: Collect all non-zero elements into a temporary vector preserving order. Overwrite the array's first positions with these elements. Fill remaining positions with zeros. Correct and simple, but uses O(n) extra space.

title: Collect non-zero elements into a separate list
Overwrite array with non-zeros then fill remaining with zeros
Optimal in-place two-pointer approach
explanation: Use a pointer i for the next non-zero placement position, starting at 0. Pointer j scans the array. When arr[j] != 0, swap arr[i] and arr[j] and increment i. This moves non-zeros forward while naturally pushing zeros back, in O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: Pointer i for next non-zero placement and j scanning
Swap arr[i] and arr[j] when arr[j] != 0 and increment i
Preserves relative order without extra memory
Complexity, correctness and edge cases
explanation: The optimal approach is O(n) time and O(1) space. Edge cases include arrays of all zeros, no zeros, and single elements. When i==j (pointer meets scanner), the swap is a no-op (element swaps with itself) — harmless but can be skipped with an if(i!=j) guard.

title: Avoiding unnecessary swaps when i == j
Loops
explanation: Loops execute a block of code repeatedly. C++ has three loop types: for (known iteration count), while (condition-checked before each iteration), and do-while (body runs at least once). For example, for(int i=0;i<5;i++) prints numbers 0 through 4 in five iterations.

title: for loop syntax: init; condition; update
loop initialization, condition and iteration steps
Loop Control Statements
explanation: break and continue modify loop execution flow. break exits the loop entirely; continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next. For example, break in a search loop stops as soon as the target is found, saving unnecessary iterations.

title: examples to avoid infinite loops (increment before continue)
Arrays
explanation: Arrays store multiple values of the same type in contiguous memory. Declare as type name[size]. Access with zero-based indexing. 2D arrays are arrays of arrays, useful for grids and matrices. For example, int grid[3][3] stores a 3×3 matrix addressable as grid[row][col].

title: Functions: Definition and Use
explanation: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform a specific task. They reduce repetition and improve readability. A function has a return type, name, parameter list, and body. For example, int square(int x){ return x*x; } can be called from anywhere in the program.

title: Parameter Passing
explanation: C++ passes arguments to functions either by value (a copy) or by reference (the original). Pass by value is safe but creates copies; pass by reference is efficient and modifies the original. For arrays, passing the array name actually passes a pointer — modifications affect the original.

title: Pass by value: behavior and examples
Pass by reference (&): effects on originals
Passing arrays to functions (decays to pointer)
STL Overview
explanation: The Standard Template Library (STL) provides ready-made containers (vector, map, set), algorithms (sort, find), and iterators that connect them. Using STL saves time and avoids reinventing the wheel. For example, using std::sort() instead of coding your own sorting saves hours and gives a highly optimized implementation.

title: When to use STL vs custom data structures
pair Utility
explanation: pair<T1,T2> groups two values of possibly different types into a single unit. Access the first value with .first and the second with .second. For example, pair<int,string> p={1,"Alice"}; stores an ID and name together. Widely used in maps and as return type for two-value results.

title: Nested pairs and array of pairs
Vector Basics
explanation: vector<T> is a dynamic array that grows automatically. Unlike raw arrays, vectors handle memory management. Access is O(1) by index; append is O(1) amortized. For example, vector<int> v; v.push_back(5); v.push_back(10); gives a vector of [5,10] without needing to declare a fixed size.

title: Declaration, dynamic sizing and memory model
Access methods: operator[], at(), front(), back()
Iterators and Traversal
explanation: Iterators are pointer-like objects that point to elements in a container. They enable algorithms to work with any container. begin() points to the first element; end() points one past the last. For example, for(auto it=v.begin();it!=v.end();it++) cout<<*it; prints all vector elements.

title: Range-based for loops and iterator-based loops
Vector Modifiers and Utilities
explanation: Vectors offer rich modification operations beyond push_back. You can insert at any position, erase elements, clear the vector, check size, and more. For example, v.erase(v.begin()+2) removes the element at index 2, shifting all subsequent elements left — O(n) operation.

title: erase() for single and range deletions
Overview
explanation: This topic introduces the two main ways English talks about the future: 'going to' and 'will be.' Both describe events that have not happened yet, but they carry different meanings. For example, 'She is going to present the report' shows a plan, while 'It will be ready soon' shows a result. Key time words like 'tomorrow' and 'next year' help signal which future form fits best.

title: Use to describe actions/events expected in the future
Distinction: recent (near) future vs later (longer-term) future
Recent future and later future
explanation: This topic draws a clear line between events happening very soon and those happening much later. 'Going to' works well for both, but time words help the reader understand distance. Example: 'I am going to submit the form tomorrow' (near) vs 'We are going to open a new branch in two years' (later). Understanding this distinction improves precision in writing.

title: Examples showing time reference (tomorrow, next year)
Future simple (going to) — Affirmative
explanation: This topic focuses on making positive future sentences using 'going to.' The structure is simple: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb. Example: 'They are going to renovate the library.' You use this form when you have already made a decision or a plan is clearly visible.

title: Structure: am/is/are + going to + base verb
Future simple (going to) — Negative
explanation: To express that something will NOT happen, insert 'not' after the am/is/are. Example: 'She is not going to attend the conference.' Contractions like 'isn't' and 'aren't' are common in spoken English and informal writing. This form expresses refusal, lack of intention, or a cancelled plan.

title: Structure: am/is/are + not + going to + base verb
Expressing refusal or lack of intention
Future simple (will be) — Future state/result
explanation: This topic covers 'will be' for describing a future condition, result, or state. Unlike 'going to,' 'will be' does not require a pre-existing plan — it is often a prediction or expectation. Example: 'The report will be ready by Friday.' It can also describe passive results: 'The bridge will be completed next month.'

title: Structure for result/passive: will + be + past participle
Structure for future states: will + be + adjective
Affirmative and negative uses focusing on result/condition
Conversation examples & usage notes
explanation: This topic brings future tense to life through real dialogue. Conversations show how 'going to' and 'will be' are actually used in daily situations — asking about plans, making predictions, or announcing decisions. A key note: 'I am going to the market' uses 'going to' as a movement verb, NOT as a future tense structure.

title: Dialog examples for plans, predictions, and decisions
Note: 'going to' followed by noun is not future tense
Distinguishing intentions (going to) vs predicted states (will be)
Definition
explanation: Ability means the power or skill to do something. 'Able to' expresses this across different time periods. It is more formal than 'can' and easier to use in all tenses. Example: In a job application you might write 'I am able to manage multiple projects simultaneously' rather than 'I can manage.'

title: Meaning of 'able to' to express ability
Applies across present, past, and future contexts
Present ability — Affirmative
explanation: For present ability, use am/is/are + able to + base verb. This shows current skill or capability. Example: 'The team is able to handle large datasets.' Use 'am' with I, 'is' with he/she/it, and 'are' with you/we/they.

title: Structure: am/is/are + able to + base verb
Present ability — Negative
explanation: To say someone cannot do something right now, use am not / is not / are not + able to + base verb. Example: 'He is not able to attend the meeting today.' This is more formal and polished than simply saying 'can't.'

title: Structure: am not / is not / are not + able to + verb
Usage and exam tips
explanation: Exams often ask you to choose between 'able to' and 'can,' or to spot subject-verb agreement errors. Use 'able to' in formal writing and across all tenses. 'Can' works only in the present. Example: Formal report — 'The system is able to process 10,000 requests per second' sounds better than 'The system can process...'

title: When to use 'able to' vs 'can' (formality and tense)
Overview
explanation: This topic introduces the idea that continuous tenses capture actions mid-stream. Unlike simple tenses that state facts, continuous tenses zoom in on a moment. Example: 'I eat lunch at 1 pm' (simple — habit) vs 'I am eating lunch right now' (continuous — in progress). The -ing ending is the defining feature.

title: Modal verb overview
explanation: This topic gives you the big picture. Modals sit before a base verb and modify its meaning. Example: 'You must submit the form' (obligation) vs 'You may leave early' (permission). The same base verb takes on completely different meanings with different modals.

title: Common modals: can, could, may, might, will, would, shall, should, must
Functions: ability, possibility, permission, requests, advice, necessity
May / Might
explanation: May and might both express possibility, but may is slightly more confident. May is also used for giving or asking permission in formal contexts. Example: 'It may rain this afternoon' (possible but uncertain). 'Might' carries even more doubt: 'It might work, but I am not sure.' In formal settings, 'May I enter?' is the polite permission request.

title: Shall
explanation: Shall is a formal modal used mainly with I and we. It can make a promise or assertion: 'I shall finish the report by noon.' It is also used for polite offers and suggestions: 'Shall I carry that for you?' In modern usage, 'shall' is more common in British English and formal writing.

title: Should
explanation: Should is the most common modal for giving advice or making recommendations. It is softer than 'must.' Example: 'You should drink more water' is advice; 'You must stay hydrated' is obligation. Exams often ask you to convert obligation to advice using 'should.'

title: Must
explanation: Must is the strongest modal for obligation and necessity. 'Must not' shows prohibition — something that is absolutely not allowed. Example: 'All employees must wear safety gear' (obligation). 'Visitors must not touch the exhibits' (prohibition). Exams test whether you can distinguish obligation from advice.

title: Uses of Present Perfect
explanation: Present Perfect has four main uses: describing life experiences ('I have visited three countries'), recent events ('She has just called'), actions with present results ('He has broken his leg — he can not walk now'), and changes over time ('Technology has improved a lot'). Identifying which use applies helps you write the right type of sentence.

title: Form & structure
explanation: The Present Perfect is built on have/has + V3. Use 'has' for he/she/it and 'have' for I/you/we/they. For passive or state results, use have/has + been + V3. Example: 'The project has been completed.' For negatives, add 'not': 'She has not replied yet.'

title: Signal words
explanation: Certain words almost always appear with Present Perfect. Spotting them helps you choose the right tense instantly. Example: 'I have never flown business class' (never), 'Have you ever tried surfing?' (ever). These signal words are a fast clue in MCQ and error-correction questions.

title: Definition
explanation: This tense answers the question 'How long has this been happening?' It stresses the ongoing nature and duration of an activity. Example: 'He has been waiting for the bus for 20 minutes' — the waiting started 20 minutes ago and is still happening right now.

title: Use: actions that began in the past and continue now
Emphasis on ongoing activity and duration
Positive structure
explanation: Positive sentences: has been + verb-ing for singular subjects (he/she/it), have been + verb-ing for I/you/we/they. Example: 'She has been teaching at this college for five years.' Always end with the -ing form of the main verb.

title: Negative structure
explanation: To say an ongoing action has NOT been happening, insert 'not' after has/have. Structure: has not been + verb-ing / have not been + verb-ing. Example: 'He has not been attending lectures regularly.' This shows an absence of continuous action over time.

title: Singular subjects: has not been + verb-ing
Time expressions and examples
explanation: Duration markers 'for' and 'since' are almost always paired with Present Perfect Continuous. 'For' measures the length of an activity; 'since' marks its starting point. Example: 'She has been interning here for two months' (duration) vs 'She has been interning since June' (start point).

title: Duration markers used with tense (e.g., for, since)
Sample contextual examples from the text
Scenario Revision of Present Perfect + Perfect Continuous
Present Perfect — forms
explanation: Two main forms: has/have + V3 for completed actions (example: 'She has submitted the report') and has/have + been + subject complement for current states (example: 'He has been a manager for five years'). Both use V3 but serve different purposes — action vs state.

title: Has/Have + past participle (V3) for completed/recent actions
Has/Have + been + subject complement for states/roles
Present Perfect — uses
explanation: Three key uses in this unit's scenarios: (1) reporting recent completed action with a present result, (2) indicating a continuing state or role, (3) describing identity or characteristics over time. Example: 'The company has earned recognition for innovation' covers both achievement and ongoing identity.

title: Recent completed action with present effect
Indicating a state or condition continuing into the present
Describing identity, role, or characteristics over time
Present Perfect Continuous — forms and uses
explanation: This form stresses how long something has been happening. Structure: has/have been + verb-ing. Example: 'The engineers have been testing the system for the past week.' The testing is ongoing. It is especially useful in progress reports and status updates.

title: Has/Have been + verb-ing (singular vs plural forms)
Use for actions that started in the recent past and continue now
Choosing between Perfect and Perfect Continuous
explanation: The key question: is the action complete or still happening? If complete with a present result → Present Perfect. If still in progress → Present Perfect Continuous. Example: 'She has written the report' (done) vs 'She has been writing the report all morning' (still going). Both can appear in the same paragraph.

title: Use Present Perfect for completed actions/experiences and states
Use Present Perfect Continuous for ongoing/recent continuous activities
Simple Tenses
explanation: Simple tenses cover three time frames. Simple Present (eat, eats) for habits and facts. Simple Past (ate) for completed actions. Simple Future (will eat / am going to eat) for upcoming events. Each has a clear, direct structure with no -ing form. Example: 'She walks to college every day' (present habit) vs 'She walked to college yesterday' (past completed).

title: Continuous Tenses
explanation: Continuous tenses capture actions in motion across all three time frames. They all use a form of 'be' + verb-ing. Example: 'She is presenting' (now), 'She was presenting when you called' (past mid-action), 'She will be presenting at 3 pm tomorrow' (future in-progress).

title: Sentence Patterns & Auxiliaries
explanation: This topic focuses on the building blocks — auxiliaries like do/does/did, be (am/is/are/was/were), and will. Knowing which auxiliary goes with which tense is essential for negatives, questions, and passive forms. Example: 'Do you attend the lab sessions?' (Simple Present question) vs 'Are you attending the lab session?' (Present Continuous question).

title: Do / does / did for negatives and questions
Will / be going to for future forms
